result:
We cast ideas like pennies into fountains and hope the splash carries far enough to be counted as luck, but everyas never follow wish-trace so easily. Night pulls its black duvet over the city and the bulbs wink on, humbled stars that traded infinity for filament. Somewhere along the river a new song is rehearsing itself on a cracked speaker; it is only half-knowledge, the rest echo back from warehouses and bridges like conspirators nodding in agreement. I listen and translate pulses into paragraphs the way dockworkers once translated bales into paychecks—no ownership, only transfer.

Language dreams of perfect packets, yet my mouth is a leaky envelope. It rains half-meanings. I say “I love you” and mean stay awake with me, and also sorry for everything implied by the toothpaste left uncapped. We live stacked inside our pronouns, loud ghosts knocking on grammar’s thin walls. Who owns the space between we and they? A pronoun is a folding chair you carry to every conversation and still have to unfold yourself.

Consider the sidewalk cracks where maple seedlings wedge their first green apostrophes against concrete sentences nobody authorized. They survive on flirtations with sun and gutter grime, violate margins, footnote the whole grid with subversive chlorophyll. Chance botanists, their ambitions are illegible to town planners, who measure only right angles and billable hours. Yet in July you’ll look down and see a miniature forest asserting period after period of soft resistance. Revolutions begin smaller than ants and wetter than tears; they begin where no form was ever meant to hold water.

As a child I collected broken calculators. I treasured the one whose eight had lost its top loop so it looked like an unfinished infinity. I pressed 4+4 and waited for the oracle of circuitry, but it answered 0.0.0. or sometimes 777. In failure their liquid-crystal prophecies felt honest, truer than the adult assurance that numbers behave. Since then I have believed in imperfect mechanisms, gods with loose wires, hearts that divide but refuse to answer with standard remainders. Love arrives like a calculator submerged in coffee—digits drift, commas multiply, the decimal point divorces itself and behaves like a period ending a sentence neither of us agreed to begin.

Memory is a playground where all equipment has been replaced yearly except the one grass-stained sneaker caught on the chain-link. I don’t recall the rules of the games, only the panic of learning them changed at recess without notice. Same with grief: the bell rings and you discover that capture the flag is now freeze tag and the flag you guarded all noon is deemed imaginary. Your teammates are already touching lampposts yelling safe, and you are still running toward a boundary erased by grown-ups who themselves had their rules rewritten in another country of childhood. Someday a therapist will tell me evolution favors organisms that let go, that the ape who clambers off the drowning limb is the one who keeps breathing. Easy to applaud the ape, harder to console the limb.

Between midnight and one the city develops a second skeleton made entirely of delivery drivers. They glide on exhaust and dubstep, stitching shut the hunger of keyboard insomniacs. At intersections they lean on horns like organists improvising haiku to caffeine. I call them angels because they arrive bearing foil-wrapped mansuetude. Wings replaced by insulated bags, halos reduced to LED blink. One brings me soup so hot it condenses summer on my ceiling. Another drops a salad in the lobby and runs before the elevator doors recognize his guilt. In every corridor a different apocalypse is scheduled between Netflix episodes; until then we feed each other carbohydrates like resumes to loneliness and hope one counts.

My neighbor in 3B plays saxophone scales each dawn as if auditioning for the sun’s job. Notes climb the fire escape like burglars of sadness, testing which windows are left unlocked. When he ascends the flattened fifth the building exhales. When he lands on blue minor sevenths, cats untuck their tails and decide today is worth starting. I have never seen his face, only the silhouette that holds brass the way astronauts hold memory of earth: close enough to feel roundness, distant enough to crave. If we ever meet in the washing room I will confess his G-blues saved certain Tuesdays otherwise lost to spreadsheet droughts. He will nod, pretend surprise, but the truth is music already collated our renter-service file into duet pending future noise complaint.

Science says the universe expands faster every second, dark energy pulling everything apart like taffy nobody asked to stretch. Against that drift we assemble miniature gravity wells: potluck dinners,Books you lend with curved spines full of someone else’s fingernail geography, the hand that finds your knee under a table and stays because pulse is a dialect even astronomers haven’t translated into equation yet. Every hug is a petition against entropy signed by two colliding bodies who promise to reassemble scattered parts later. Some petitions are denied in advance. Still we offer, because the alternative is vacuum where even dust learns loneliness.

I want to mail you a letter containing only punctuation: commas for pauses in breathing, semicolons for shared jokes that do not want to stop talking, an em dash—because thought detours like a delivery van through neighborhoods not listed on the original dispatch. The envelope will weigh nothing, yet take three stamps. The post office will suspect contraband optimism. Opening it will feel like unfolding road maps written on fog, but somewhere between the parentheses you’ll remember the approximate temperature of my wrist against yours on a couch that has since become landfill. Punctuation marks are fossils of gestures, and this crusty question mark that curls like a cat might once have been my shoulder nudging yours awake when the credits rolled and neither of us knew how to end the movie politely so we invented sequel indefinitely.

Let us therefore praise partially: the half-erased whiteboard floral of someone brainstorming hope; the street drummer whose rhythm limps yet makes toddlers sway like metronomes; the grandmother who forgets your name but never the recipe for the soup that remedies unnamed aches. Praise the parcel tracker that never updates yet still delivers eventually. Praise garden hoses coiled like softened days, and lightning bugs teaching dusk dimmer-switch lessons. Praise the parking lot seagull inland for reasons never explained yet somehow ours to witness. Praise every imperfect organ still subjectively operational this morning—yours, mine, the planet’s wobbling gyroscope. Praise especially the sentence noticing it is ending soon; already its letters buckle toward page’s cliff. Yet see how, even here, a final period seeds tomorrow’s comma, small, black, relentless, growing into an apostrophe that insists there’s still more belonging to claim.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
